coredata.dc:title: A data-driven equivalent circuit model’s parameter estimation method applied to Lithium-Titanate battery
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Maria, Enrico Dalla
Dallapiccola, Mattia
Aloisio, Davide
Brunaccini, Giovanni
Sergi, Francesco
Moser, David
Barchi, Grazia
coredata.dc:description: Lithium battery systems are becoming essential for a variety of applications, such as maximisation of self-produced renewable energy in buildings, grid balances, and electric vehicles. Accurate and equivalent models are required to study and analyse the behaviour of batteries, both static and dynamic. This paper presents a novel data-driven parameter estimation procedure for high-current battery modelling, which has been applied to experimental measurement data conducted on Lithium Titanate batteries. The proposed estimation methodology has been applied to three different equivalent circuit models characterised by increasing modelling complexity. Genetic algorithm optimisation techniques solve the optimisation problem, estimating the model’s parameters and a look-up table of battery parameters estimated in discretised State of Charge. Enhance the battery operation modelling at high depth of discharge, the model is applied to the battery operated at high currents, covering its entire domain of state of charge. The fully depleted and charged state is given increased focus to maintain good modelling performance. Unlike previous works, the proposed methodology repeats the parameters estimation procedure for more than thirty-five state of charge ranges using non-equal width ranges to increase model density in critical regions. Based on the estimation results for one C-rate, it was found that the parameter estimation error ranges from 0.17% for the Rint model to 0.03% for the Dual-Polarisation model. The validation results confirmed that the proposed approach had a normalised root mean square error lower than 2%. The proposed methodology encompasses the entire process of parameter estimation, starting from raw experimental data. As such, it can be generalised and applied to other battery technologies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Battery modelling
Equivalent circuit model
Genetic algorithm
Parameter estimation

The authors thank the Department of Innovation, Research and University of the Autonomous Province of Bozen/Bolzano for covering the Open Access publication costs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Overall different discharge C-rates test, voltage–current profile.
Voltage vs capacity curves at different discharge C-rates.
HPPC test, voltage–current profile, with an inset of the discharge-regen pulses phase.
SoC-OCV relation extracted from 1C pulses HPPC test.

Each image represents a phase of the data pre-processing. Fig. 5(a) reports an example of the original HPPC test measured battery potential profile (id3 of Table 2). Fig. 5(b) shows the subset of data that are extracted (the colours highlights each subset, obtained according to the available SOC ranges). Fig. 5(c) focus on a single subset of data (in this case shown SoC range is 0.95–0.94). It contains the two pulses (charge and discharge) and three idle time before, in between and after these events. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Diagram of the parameters estimation method. Starting from the inputs: data and the mathematical statement of the equivalent circuit model. The core of the process is the parameters estimation phase. The parameters are identified by exploiting the Genetic Algorithm and therefore iterating till reaching a satisfactory reduction of the cost function that evaluates the distance between the original data and the model guess. The output of the process is the estimated model parameters organised in a look-up table.

Parameters of the Thevenin model estimated with different datasets. Respectively
U
O C

Parameters of the Thevenin model estimated with different datasets. Focus on the polarising RC: from above,

. The parameters refer to the equation reported in Table 1 Eq. (2). Fig. 8(c) reports the legend that holds for all the sub-figures above.

Convergence of the GA-based parameters estimation algorithm for id3 dataset, Thevenin EC model. Each line colour represents the single parameter estimation instance, while each point the est cost function value at each generation of the GA algorithm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Estimation NRMSE [%] (each point represents the NRMSE of the single sub-model relative to a SOC range). From top downward, the sub-figures report the results respectively of the Rint, Thevenin, and DP equivalent circuit model estimation. The line colour is consistent with the exploited dataset. Refer to Table 5 for a summary of the NRMSE for each combination of dataset times model. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Circuit and expressions of the three explored ECMs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: List of tests and datasets considered for the model’s parameter estimation procedure and the relative validation.
Definition of θ , vector of parameters, for each equivalent circuit model. Refer to Table 1 for more details related to ECM definitions.
List of test and datasets considered for the model’s parameter estimation procedure.
Mean parameter estimation Normalised Root-Mean-Square-Error for each SoC range for the explored dataset of Table 4 and the three explored ECM models of Table 1. Values are reported in percentage [%].
Mean parameters estimation time in seconds (s) for each SoC range for the explored datasets of Table 2 and the three explored ECM models of Table 1.

NRMSE values expressed in percentage resulting from the validation of the obtained model of the available dataset of Table 2. The applied colourmap provides also a graphical comparison between the reported results. Each row is an estimated model, while the columns contain the results according to the dataset adopted in the validation phase.

head.ce:title: A data-driven equivalent circuit model’s parameter estimation method applied to Lithium-Titanate battery

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lithium battery systems are becoming essential for a variety of applications, such as maximisation of self-produced renewable energy in buildings, grid balances, and electric vehicles. Accurate and equivalent models are required to study and analyse the behaviour of batteries, both static and dynamic. This paper presents a novel data-driven parameter estimation procedure for high-current battery modelling, which has been applied to experimental measurement data conducted on Lithium Titanate batteries. The proposed estimation methodology has been applied to three different equivalent circuit models characterised by increasing modelling complexity. Genetic algorithm optimisation techniques solve the optimisation problem, estimating the model’s parameters and a look-up table of battery parameters estimated in discretised State of Charge. Enhance the battery operation modelling at high depth of discharge, the model is applied to the battery operated at high currents, covering its entire domain of state of charge. The fully depleted and charged state is given increased focus to maintain good modelling performance. Unlike previous works, the proposed methodology repeats the parameters estimation procedure for more than thirty-five state of charge ranges using non-equal width ranges to increase model density in critical regions. Based on the estimation results for one C-rate, it was found that the parameter estimation error ranges from 0.17% for the Rint model to 0.03% for the Dual-Polarisation model. The validation results confirmed that the proposed approach had a normalised root mean square error lower than 2%. The proposed methodology encompasses the entire process of parameter estimation, starting from raw experimental data. As such, it can be generalised and applied to other battery technologies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:def-description.ce:para: Expected battery potential at time i

RC time constant
τ
t h
=
R
t h
C
t h
Measured battery potential at time i

ce:section.ce:para: The continuous evolution of the energy sector towards a green, sustainable, and necessary transformation is highlighting, more and more, the importance of storage technologies in supporting renewable energy sources penetration and keeping the security of supply during power network operation.

Among the various electrochemical storage technologies, lithium batteries (LiB) became rapidly in the last years the most dominant [1] thanks to their high performances in terms of energy and power density, cycle life and, recently, low capex costs [2]. Lithium Titanate has been chosen as electrochemical technology in this work, because it assures a really high cycle life (
> 20 . 000
cycles), an excellent fast response to renewables unbalances and on–off operation of heat pumps, high C-rated both in charge and discharge with moderate internal temperature change, safer behaviour [3] and more thermal abuse tolerance [4] than graphite-based lithium batteries. This kind of LiB uses a different anode than other Li-ion batteries, which usually exploit graphite-based anode, the lithium titanate oxide Li4Ti5O12 (LTO), and a cathode (NMC, LMO, LFP) depending on the manufacturer choice. The Lithium titanate anode avoids the SEI (Solid Electrolyte Interface) formation and has low volume expansion during intercalation processes improving the lifetime and, thanks to a higher potential vs Li/Li+, can offer superior safety features by eliminating the production of any type of dendrite formation [5]. As a consequence, however, the different anode brings to a lower cell voltage with respect to the other LiB technologies and thus a lower energy density. That said, the LTO technology has many strengths that become key features in the main applications envisioned in the literature reviewed. Several papers explore the potential of this battery technology in different applications: in [6,7] good power performances were applied to automotive applications both in fully electric powertrain and hybrid solutions [8], in [9] the impact of high discharge C-rate on ageing was investigated. In [10,11] the high stability and long life were considered for ancillary application in the stationary sector. [9] envision both the two already reviewed application scenarios in [9]: the adoption in the vehicle industry and stationary energy storage. [12] focus in [12] on additional key characteristics: the safety and stability in a wide range of operating temperatures (both at low and high temperatures) compared to a concurrent technology. There, the target is high-power automotive applications, but the same concern covers all application scenarios. The automotive sector application recurs also in [8,13], where the authors propose the adoption of a hybrid battery pack storage for e-vehicle applications to search for a trade-off design considering energy and power constraints.

The implementation of storage technologies is often assisted by the development of models able to simulate the battery behaviour. Many research activities were carried out in the development of such models [14–18]. A first important distinction among them is reported in [17] in which three main categories were identified:

ce:list-item.ce:para: mechanistic models, which use mathematical equations, based on physical and chemical knowledge, regarding the processes directly occurring inside the battery;
equivalent circuit and impedance models which try to describe the dynamic behaviour of the system by means of simple combinations of electrical elements representing electrochemical processes;
data-driven models which consider the system as a black box and try to extrapolate significant features able to describe the system.

ce:section.ce:para: Equivalent circuit models are the most conventional methods adopted to model the behaviour of lithium batteries. They use a combination of standard electrical components to provide a simplified model of the complex phenomena that combined compose the observed battery behaviour. Moreover, they offer an easy understanding of the electrical characteristic of the battery [19] while allowing the researchers to design with sufficient freedom the circuit structure more suitable for their application, a peculiar battery technology, or the adopted parameter estimation technique. Several circuit topologies are reviewed and validated in literature [18–22]. A similar analysis holds in the considered literature concerning specifically the battery technology adopted in this work lithium titanate oxide (LTO) [20,23].

The circuit models more applied and known in literature are the Rint model, the resistive Thevenin and the second order RC Thevenin model or Dual-Polarisation (DP) [18]. 1
1
Table 1 reports the reviewed literature references that concern the equivalent circuit model selected for the current work. More details follow in Section 2.1. In general, an ECM that includes at least one RC network is able to provide a description of the dynamic behaviour of the battery characteristics [10,20,24].

Within this context, this work aims to propose a new procedure for the estimation of the parameters of the battery equivalent circuit models (ECMs) by means of data-driven model based on genetic algorithm optimisation performed over measured data. Similarly to the solutions proposed in [20] and [25], the resulting model is composed by the mathematical formulation of the ECM and by a look-up table with the values of the model parameters for each range of State of Charge (SoC) considered. We compare the impact of factors (several C-rates and three different but most commonly adopted EC models) and we conclude by proposing a subset of the originally explored factors with the aim of drawing a standardised procedure for future application to different technologies and products. In this regard, the selected battery technology is relatively innovative among the several variations of LiB technologies. This technology is characterised by important operating parameters, such as high currents, negligible calendar ageing and the capability to operate the device down to low values of SoC compared to other technologies. Operating potentially at such a high depth of discharge (DoD) requires maintaining a good modelling capability in the whole range of SoC (from fully charged state to almost completely depleted status). A good modelling capability may finally bring to the exploration of an extension of the operative DoD also for the other battery technologies. This requirement forces us to focus also on the extreme regions of SoCs. Our methodology repeats the parameters estimation procedure for a high number of SoC ranges (more than thirty-five) in the whole SoC domain. Unlike previous works [20,25–27], we combine in the proposed procedure a high number of estimation repetitions over the SoC domain and the non-equal distribution of samples over the considered domain. Differently from the consolidated procedure for parameter extraction [20,28–30], we opted for the introduction of heuristic optimisation tools that automate the parameter estimation phase.

This work discusses the proposed procedure and highlights its potential and limitations in combination with all the considered equivalent circuit models (Rint, Thevenin, and Dual-Polarisation) and the datasets resulting from the measurement test campaign. Despite considering only these models, the authors believe that it is possible to further extend the application of the proposed methodology to more refined EC models and their mathematical transcriptions.

The paper is structured as follows. Section 2 defines the equivalent circuit model (ECM) adopted to model the battery and the modelling assumptions. Experimental measurement and initial data processing are discussed in Section 3. After these preliminary parts, Section 4 described the proposed modelling procedure in detail. The second half of the paper presents the results of the proposed approach applied to the LTO battery case study and the actual measurement data. More in detail, Section 5 presents the estimation results, while Section 6 focuses on the validation and finally the conclusions.

Several battery modelling approaches are presented in literature as reported in [19,21,22,31]. In the current work, we adopt a modelling approach based on the Equivalent Circuit Model (ECM). ECM models the battery as a simplified electrical circuit composed by a voltage source related to the state-of-charge (SoC) and a combination of additional resistors and capacitors depending on the actual complexity of the model. Table 1 reports the definitions of the ECMs selected in the current work. The Rint model is the simplest ECM model and is composed by the voltage source and resistor in series that represents the internal battery resistance (first row of Table 1). By introducing in the circuit more Resistance–Capacitance (RC) networks it is possible to add a dynamic behaviour to the equivalent circuit. The Thevenin model and the Dual-Polarisation (DP) model introduce respectively one and two RC components in series (second and third entries of Table 1). Several other combinations of electrical circuits are proposed in literature [19,32]. They mostly rely on higher-order models with additional RC branches and different circuit topologies. Such proposed models aim to better capture the dynamics of the voltage at the battery terminals, embedding more factors that combined compose its characteristics. Nonetheless, in the current work, we focus on the three models reported in Table 1.

On top of the standard EC modelling, the proposed methodology introduces a definition of parameters (values of capacitance, resistance and open circuit voltage) that are piece-wise functions of the state of charge (SoC) estimation. This approach is similarly adopted in [31] limited to the description of the OCV characteristics and in [20,27,28] for all the model parameters identified in their equivalent circuits. Such a modelling approach assumes a different behaviour and response of the battery cell within the explored range of SoC. Different values of the model’s parameters may catch these changes still preserving the simple underlying modelling structure and formulation. The SoC enters indirectly the proposed procedure as its estimation is adopted to enforce the discretisation of the parameter values according to the SoC range. By doing so, the modelling provides several sub-models, each specific to a small range of SoC. While this component is not directly object of the procedure developments, a proper estimation improves the robustness of the proposed models in simulations undertaken to mirror real systems. Therefore, here it is shortly discussed.

The state of charge is the remaining charge of a battery normalised by the total nominal capacity. In the current work the Ampere-Hour integral method (i.e., Coulomb-counting) [26] is selected as the SoC estimation algorithm.
(4)
S o C = S o
C
0
−
1
C
N
∫
t
t
0
i
( t )
⋅ d t
where
C
N
is the nominal capacity (Ah) and
S o
C
0
is the initial value of the state of charge. The Coulomb-counting method is the simplest approach for SoC estimation, but it may be sensible to initialisation biases and estimation drifts due to cumulative effects [36]. Moreover, this simplified approach neglects any operating states of the battery (e.g. C-rate, temperature, SoH, Peukert effect) that may affect the battery performance actually experienced in a real application. As here the focus is the ECM parameters estimation procedure, this SoC evaluation approach is still kept despite the known limitations with room for improvement.

The proposed methodology explores two factors that alter the battery behaviour: State of Charge and C-rate. Other phenomena (e.g. ageing) and factors (such as temperature) are not included in the proposed model and cannot be addressed using the datasets produced from the experimental tests carried out by the authors. For the type of campaign test carried out in this work, no relevant ageing effect was detected. According to the results reported in [10], such a campaign should require an extended repetition of the selected tests either during the entire life of the device (thousands of cycles) or in combination with proper ageing tests as described in [28,37,38].

Several phenomena contribute to the degradation of the characteristics of LiB batteries [39,40]. They are typically described by the State of Health (SoH) parameter that includes [41]. Lifetime degradation results in increased internal resistance and decreased capacity retention. It is common practice to identify two components that contribute to the capacity fade: calendar (under rest state) and cyclic ageing (under usage state). Separate models can be deployed to reproduce the two contributions that can be merged by the simple addition of the two capacity losses exploiting the superposition principle [37]. Moreover, the particular technology studied (LTO) is distinguished by a substantially negligible calendar ageing as revealed by the test campaign described in [11,42], especially considering typical lifetimes of other lithium batteries. In addition, in [10], the same technology was tested in collaboration with the Italian TSO (Terna) to evaluate the ageing effect of stationary cycling on it. Among all the lithium technologies tested, LTO showed a very low percentage of capacity reduction over thousands of cycles.

The second factor subject to simplification in this work is the effect of the temperature. Regarding temperature hysteresis in the OCV behaviour, in [11] [11] report the SoC-OCV curves of the tested LTO cell [11, Figure 5] showing small differences in the SoC-OCV curve behaviour at different temperatures. A similar observation leads the authors of [7] to opt for models built on a constant OCV over SoC characteristic that is related to nominal temperature (25°C) and C-rate (1C).

The LTO technology shows a small reduction in the actual capacity at different temperatures and C-rate [11]. This phenomenon has been investigated in [11, Figure 7] where the recorded reduction is only of 0.1% from 1C up to 4C. In addition, LTO technology proved to have good performance in terms of round-trip efficiency and capacity release [43]. For all these reasons reviewed in the literature, we proceeded with the simplification of the modelling as described previously, leaving our focus on the role of C-rate and SoC.

The last mention concerns the work of [7], who provide in [7] an extended test campaign of several LTO technologies for modelling purposes. Their work reports a small hysteresis effect and a reduced temperature impact on open circuit voltage (can be neglected if maximum deviations of 1% SoC can be accepted). However, at very boundary conditions the provided modelling needs to be upgraded by additional elements to obtain an accurate voltage simulation [7].

The mathematical formulation presented in Section 2.1 (and in detail in Table 1) lends itself to being potentially implemented with any scientific programming language. Only some of the work cited in this paper specifies the adopted programming language. Matlab/Simulink [19,25,26,33–35] is the most used combination of languages, while in [44] [44] explore the adoption of Python with the aim of providing an open-source framework for battery modelling. Such languages typically offer a large and powerful set of libraries for data management, processing, computation, and finally to interface with real-world hardware. In our case, we selected this last option (i.e. Python) to model the ECM and to perform all the pre and post-processing analysis.

The development of a robust model needs the building of a proper test campaign, able to provide the necessary amount of significant data for training, analysis and feature extraction of the model itself. In particular, experiences on field coming from the research sector, battery manufacturers and final users suggest a precise test campaign development, (constituted by a set of typical tests) able to completely characterise the battery behaviour, both from a long-period point of view and real-time response. The experimental results obtained from this campaign are therefore reported in the following section.

The battery adopted as the case study is a 23Ah lithium titanate oxide Li4Ti5O12 (LTO) cell with a nominal voltage of 2.3V. The tests performed were carried out at standard environmental conditions, in a climatic chamber 2
2 Climatic chamber model Angelantoni DY 600, able to work in a temperature range of -35°C to 180°C and a humidity range of 10% to 98%. with controlled thermohygrometric environment. During tests, only the temperature control was activated (T = 25°C).

A high-power battery cycler, able to request power until 1000 A (500 A for each of two channels, parallelable) in a range of 0–20 V (model Bitrode FTV-1 1000/100/10-20) was used to implement the test cycles. The cycler is capable of an accuracy of ± 0.1% FS which becomes respectively in the adopted operation ranges 0.01A (10A max range) and 0.1A (100A max range). Voltage accuracy is 0.02V in the adopted range 0–20 V.

Measurements were carried out internally to the cycler with a time resolution of 0.1s. All the measurements performed guarantee high accuracy thanks to the calibration carried out periodically on the instruments used. In addition, the use of a controlled environment allows a high repeatability of selected working conditions and optimal measurement extraction.

The first characterisation tests were performed to extract charge and discharge profiles at different C-rates. Generally, C-rate is an important parameter both in terms of real-time performances, showing the goodness of the storage system in terms of power response (in particular, this is a strong point for LTO technology, which normally offers high C-rates both in charge and in discharge with a stable thermal behaviour), and overall lifetime, because it normally constitutes a main ageing factor, especially in case of high C-rates values. The different C-rates discharge test is constituted by various galvanostatic discharges (0.5C, 1C, 2C, 3C, corresponding to 11.5 A, 23 A, 46 A, 69 A, respectively) performed until the lower cut-off voltage is reached. The battery is first recharged every time at nominal current (galvanostatic mode at 23 A) until the upper cut-off voltage, and then finely fully charged by using a potentiostatic phase locked at the cut-off voltage until the value of current went below the threshold of 0.1 A. The battery is then relaxed with a one-hour rest phase before the next discharge step is started. In the same way, a test for evaluation of battery charge at different C-rates was carried out by changing the charging current value each time (0.5C, 1C, 2C, 3C) and discharging at nominal current. At the end of each galvanostatic charge, the potenstiostatic phase at the upper cutoff voltage was always performed to fully recharge the battery. Fig. 1 shows the output of the whole test performed for battery characterisation at different discharge C-rates in terms of voltage and current responses. In particular, the voltage–capacity curves for each discharge condition were extracted and highlighted in Fig. 2, where the results show the decreasing voltage profile with increasing C-rate (normal behaviour due to higher internal losses) but also how the total capacity (Ah) extracted remains practically unchanged as the C-rate increases.

The second type of test carried out is the Hybrid Power Pulse Characterisation test (HPPC). This kind of test is intended to characterise the dynamic power capability using a test profile that incorporates both discharge and regen pulses, by establishing also its relationship with the depth of discharge parameter (DoD) [45]. In addition, performing these pulses at different SoC levels, the voltage values at the end of the discharge regeneration are directly related to the system round-trip efficiency. Therefore, it is evident why the HPPC is so important in providing information regarding the dynamic battery behaviour. This is particularly true for model development, due to the possibility of extracting the fixed ohmic cell resistance (directly related to the voltage instantaneous variations between the various operational phases) and cell polarisation resistance as a function of SoC. Also, the battery response time (linked to the voltage response time constants) and the degradation behaviour during lifetime (all extracted parameters change with the battery ageing) can be evaluated. Finally, due to the particular profile used, which, at each SoC value, provides a long rest phase for the stabilisation of the battery parameters, a well-defined SoC-OCV profile (very important for SoC calculation algorithms and in general to understand the battery behaviour) can be extracted. An overview of the overall HPPC test with an inset of one of the various discharge and regen pulses performed at different SoCs is shown Fig. 3. Here it is well visible what happens at each defined SoC level. The applied HPPC test protocol is described:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Start condition: thermal stability at a temperature of 25°C and fully charged cell.

Repetition until fully discharged:
(a)
Execution of discharge pulse at a fixed C-rate. The pulse duration is 10s.
(b)
Follow a one-minute rest phase to evaluate rapid relaxation evolution.
(c)
Execution of a charge pulse at a fixed C-rate and the relative rest phase of 1min duration.
(d)
A capacity reduction step brings the battery to the next SoC value.
(e)
Follow a long rest phase of 2h to evaluate the static equilibrium status.
Galvanostatic Charge followed by potentiostatic charge according to respectively voltage and current stop criteria defined by the cell manufacturer.

ce:section.ce:para: SoC steps analysed with the HPPC test are chosen considering that high and low-capacity value zones normally show higher variation. For this reason, the test is performed with 1% SoC variation between 100%–90% and 10%–0% SoC ranges, while with 5% SoC variation in the remaining SoC range (90%–10%). The HPPC was repeated with different values of C-rate pulses (more specifically, 0.2C, 0.5C, 1C, 3C). Fig. 4 shows an extraction of a SoC-OCV relation obtained using HPPC test with 1C pulses. Here the voltage decreasing profile around the 2.3 V nominal value for LTO cells is visible with a typical “hump” of the LTO technology (close to 60% SoC), normally associated to a phase change of the cathode [46]. The SoC-OCV curve is generally used (in combination with other parameters) in SoC calculation algorithms implemented in BMS (Battery Management System). The list of all the tests performed is shown in Table 2. It is important to underline that all the HPPC tests (id1..id4 from 0.2C to 3C) were used to test the model’s parameters estimation methodology (Section 5, while the complete charge and discharge cycles (indicates as CHADIS or id0) were used for model validation (Section 6).
The data gathered from the presented test campaign requires preliminary processing before the actual parameter estimation takes place.

At first, the aim of this step is to remove all the parts of the test that are required to initialise the battery and move it to the required states. The initial and final charge phases are removed (see the first and last section of the characteristic provided in Fig. 3). That trajectories are required to initialise the test, first bringing the battery to its maximum SoC and then recovering from the fully depleted state reached at the end of the procedure. A similar treatment is also reserved to all the intermediate discharge operations induced to bring the cell at each selected SoC value and to the following idle state that allows the cell to recover internal equilibrium after the induced perturbation. These parts of the test are functional to its execution but are not required for the parameter estimation, which will focus on the response to the couple of pulses applied to the battery.

The point reached after the initial charge transient is kept as the reference for the initialisation of the SoC estimation of the battery. At that point, the battery is at its complete charge state and the estimation of the SoC described in Eq. (4) is there initialised at SoC = 100%.

The final point of the characteristics is considered the point with SoC equal to 0%. According to the two fixed points and the available applied current profile, it is possible to evaluate a-posteriori the total extracted capacity from the battery during the whole HPPC test. Obtained the total exchanged capacity, it is possible to estimate the SoC variation according to Eq. (4). Given the highly controlled condition characteristic of the test, it is so possible to compute the SoC relatively to these two fixed points mitigating the drawbacks of the Coulomb-counting approach introduced in Section 2.2.

On the base of the estimated SoC, the input data are sliced according to the interval considered in the HPPC test. Doing so, we produce a subset of data containing the two pulses applied for each range of SoC. As described in Section 3.3, the HPPC curve is composed of multiple pulses extracted and highlighted by the colours in Fig. 5(b). The pulses are executed in couple for each SoC range, respectively charging and discharging the battery with a small idle time to expose the rest behaviour relief dynamics after the pulse perturbation. The pair of pulses is repeated for each range of the SoC identified. After each test, the SoC of the battery is lowered to the next required level. Next, a long rest phase follow. These latter two phases of the profile are not required for the parameter estimation, therefore the procedure will cut the entire HPPC test profile into small subsets containing the two pulses with the behaviour of the battery immediately before and after the pulses Fig. 5(b). This window of time before and after the pulses is kept equivalent to the length of the idle phase that is held between the charge and discharge pulse. Fig. 5(c) proposes an example of the final resulting profile.
The data pre-processing phase takes care to extract only the point relevant for the derivation of the RC parameters.

The amount of data that enters the estimation is relevant as the actual parameter estimation is done minimising a cost function computed on those measured points. In this regard, it is relevant to grant the presence in the input dataset of both the response to the forcing pulses, the recover phase (both relevant for the dynamic behaviour of the device), and finally the behaviour at idle (kept from the characteristic immediately before the application of the first pulse). These three components should be present with a comparable number of samples for a better estimation of the model and a comparable weight in the cost function evaluation. This may improve a comparable performance of the final model in all the three regions of operation.

Fig. 5 provides a final summary of the data pre-processing phases on a sample dataset related to the HPPC test. Starting from the raw data of Fig. 5(a), the subset of data of interest is extracted for each available SoC range. Fig. 5(b) shows all the subset of data adopted for the following parameter estimation phase, while Fig. 5(c) focuses on a single subset composed of the two pulses and the idle time. This operation allows to extract a reduced amount of data for each SoC range, still keeping the original fine time resolution, required to appreciate the actual response during the pulses that are characterised by a very short duration.

Fig. 6 contains a graphical summary of the proposed parameters estimation methodology. The mathematical formulation of the model described and a set of experimental measurements presented in the previous sections are the inputs. The core of the process, i.e. the parameters estimation phase, is identified by exploiting the Genetic Algorithm (GA) and therefore iterating till reaching a satisfactory reduction of the cost function that evaluates the distance between the original data and the model guess. The output of the process is the estimated model parameters organised in a look-up table that are reported separately for each identified range of state of charge estimation.

The parameter to be estimated depends on the model selected. In the current work, we explore the three models presented in Table 1. The parameters of the Rint model are
U
O C
and
R
0
, where
U
O C
represents the open circuit voltage (OCV) of the cell and
R
0
is the internal equivalent ohmic resistance. In addition to them, the Thevenin and the DP model require also the parameters related to the RC components that are respectively
R
t h 1
and
C
t h 1
(Thevenin) and
R
t h 1 , 2
and
C
t h 1 , 2
(DP model).
R
t h 1 , 2
and
C
t h 1 , 2
are the equivalent resistance and the polarising capacity for the RC networks and provide the dynamic response in the circuit. The process used for the parameters estimation consists in the minimisation of the difference between the measured potential curve and the one obtained with the model. Eq. (5) provides the statement of the optimisation problem
(5)
min
θ
∑
i = 1
N
u
L , i
−
u
ˆ
L , i
2
N
1 / 2
(6)
s.t.
θ
m i n
≤ θ ≤
θ
m a x
where N is the number of samples,
u
L , i
=
u
L
(
t
i
)
is the measured battery potential, and
u
ˆ
L , i
=
u
ˆ
L
( θ ,
t
i
)
is the model evaluated in θ , vector of the parameters. Table 3 reports the definition of the vector θ for each explored equivalent circuit model with the same schema adopted in Table 1.

The algorithm used to solve the minimisation algorithm is a Genetic Algorithm implemented in pymoo [47]. Genetic Algorithms (GA) are meta-heuristic techniques that belong to the class of evolutionary algorithms (Process flowchart in the box on the right of Fig. 6). The algorithm is initialised with an initial population sampled in the admissible range of the parameters. For each individual, the model is evaluated by calculating the error between the measured and modelled curves. The cost function is kept as a criterion for the selection of the member of the population. The survival of the individuals with the better fitness is typically researched. Starting from this subset of the original populations, a new generation is built crossing the original members and introducing mutations. In other words, the algorithm combines the values of the parameters with better results, introducing an additional perturbation to grant a certain amount of robustness against the fall into local minima. This process is repeated for G generations and is halted when the algorithms produce no more significant improvements in its proceeding (convergence) or a maximum number of generations is reached.
In the current work, no constraints relating each sub-model specific to a SoC range are explored actively in the estimation phase.

The estimation is performed independently for each range of SoC defined by
S o
C
m i n
, S o
C
m a x
, where
S o
C
m i n
and
S o
C
m a x
are respectively the lower and upper values of the range (
S o
C
m i n
< S o
C
m a x
). Thus, each parameter may be different for each SoC interval. Finally, the result of the parameter estimation procedure is a table of parameters defined for each SoC interval.

This section reports the results of the application of the parameter estimation procedure to the measurement datasets from the single battery cell. Then, we will discuss the performance of the proposed GA-based parameters estimation procedure applied to the explored equivalent circuit model variants and restricted to the specific case study and the relative measurement datasets. We report in Table 4 the subset of datasets used for the estimation phase based on the complete Table 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimation of the parameters of the single cell
ce:section.ce:para:
The genetic algorithm is selected as the optimisation technique and it is implemented in Python programming language (version 3.8.10) using the library pymoo [47] (version 0.5.0). Its population number is set to 100 individuals. The algorithm requires as input also the ranges in which to search for the optimal parameter values. Those ranges are therefore defined by fixing a lower and upper value. The following values are adopted in the current work: for the equivalent resistance (
R
0
) value, the range 1 × 10-5 ohm to 0.1ohm is adopted. The equivalent capacitance (
C
t h
) boundaries are 5 × 102 F to 5 × 106 F. Those values are derived from a concurrent process of iteration of parameter estimation in an early development phase, literature Refs. [27], and the outcomes of the parameters extraction from the magnified I,V profile during the charge and discharge pulses according to the procedure described in [26,28–30]. The open-circuit voltage (OCV) range is fixed according to batteries nominal value, the adopted range is from
0 . 6 ⋅
V
n o m
and
1 . 4 ⋅
V
n o m
. The aim of the optimisation is to identify within the given range the value of each model’s parameters that minimise the provided cost function (Eq. (5)).

Before entering the parameter estimation phase, data are pre-processed to extract the actual subset of information useful for the estimation procedure. Section 3.4 previously introduces this data preparation step. The estimation procedure described in Section 4 is repeated for each entry of Table 4. The output of each estimation is a look-up table with the model parameters for each tested SoC range.

Figs. 7 and 8 report some of the resulting estimated parameters, mostly focusing on the Thevenin model. The estimated open-circuit voltage values (Fig. 7(a)) are consistent among the different considered datasets with an almost linear behaviour for the SoC from 0.1 and 0.9 and a change in slope at the beginning and end of the SoC ranges. Such behaviour is slightly more pronounced for the dataset related to the measurement performed in the HPPC with a higher C-rate. The ohmic resistance
R
0
(Fig. 7(b)) exhibits higher values near the beginning and the end of the curve similar to what was observed by [27,28], while having almost a constant value in the range of SoC between 0.1 and 0.9. A higher offset may be observed between the different datasets.

In the discussion of the results obtained, we must consider that the GA identifies the parameters according to the minimisation of the provided cost function in a wide range of values. GA provides therefore the better solution compared to the others explored and may therefore be trapped in local minima. This issue is overall relevant if we look at the position of the resistance and capacitance contribution both in Eqs. (2) and (3), where
R
t h 1 , 2
and
C
t h 1 , 2
are present at the denominator both as a product (
R
t h 1 , 2
⋅
C
t h 1 , 2
as reported in Fig. 8(c)) and alone
C
t h 1 , 2
. Moreover, they are searched in a very wide range of values (the width is almost
1
0
4
) and which are on average several orders of magnitude apart. Fig. 8 finally reports the values estimated for the first polarising network. Both
R
t h 1 , 2
and
C
t h 1 , 2
(respectively Fig. 8(a) and 8(b) are mostly constant in the range of SoC from 0.1 to 0.9. In the regions at the margins of the SoC domain, they respectively show a slight increase (
R
t h 1 , 2
) and a small decrease (
C
t h 1 , 2
) in their value.

Fig. 8(c) finally reports their product expressed as time constant
τ
t h 1
=
R
t h 1
C
t h 1
. In the GA case, the values are mostly constant in the whole range of SoC. Whereas, the results in the first range of SoC near the fully charged condition have to be considered outliers and are connected to a rough estimation of the model parameters in this first subset of data in
S o C ≈ 1
. This choice is not critical as we have a dense discretisation of the characteristics in that region and the parameters estimated in the near ranges of SoC may replace the ones marked as outliers. Moreover, having many narrower ranges of SoC and therefore a more dense modelling of the characteristic in more critical regions, makes the model more robust to errors in the estimation procedure. We evaluate the estimation parameters also in Rint and DP, but the observations of the final outcome in the current work suggest the adoption of the Thevenin model as a better trade-off between complexity, performance, and ease of parameter estimation (similarly to [21,22]). For this reason, we report those results explicitly.

The GA algorithm works iteratively looking for an improvement of the cost function generation after generation. It is terminated according to two concurrent criteria: no more significant improvement of the cost function (5) in the latest generations, and a maximum number of generations (in the current case this value is set to 50, a value identified according to the behaviour of the algorithm applied to the available data). As regards the first termination criterion, a tolerance value of 0.001 is considered in a sliding window evaluated every 5 generations. Fig. 9 shows the convergence of the best cost-function values for the parameter estimation done on id3 data with the Thevenin model. Most of the optimisations converge within the suggested improvement thresholds before the maximum number of generations is reached.

For the reported example, most of the algorithm improvements are produced in the first iterations (generation 0 to 10) while only small improvements can be observed after. The other combination of models and data sources exhibit similar behaviour.

Eq. (5) (Section 4) states the definition of the cost function that proposes an evaluation of the distance between the estimated model and the original subset of measurements. This value is further elaborated to present here a benchmark of the model’s performance resulting from the estimation procedure. Table 5 contains the mean value of the NRMSE for each combination of the equivalent circuit model and source dataset. The Rint model performs worse in all the explored datasets. Inspecting the obtained results, it is possible to observe that the proposed procedure is able to estimate the parameters of the Rint model to represent mostly the only OCV value of the battery. Similar to the results of [22], the procedure is repeated estimating the parameters with only the point with a value of current different from zero (therefore removing also the idle time before, between and after the pulses) but no significant improvements are observed. The focus moves on the two more complex ECMs. The DP model performs slightly better than the simpler Thevenin model. This observation holds for the parameter estimation on the tests id3,4 datasets with marginal improvements suggested by the NRMSE metric. For the datasets id1,2, the performances of Thevenin and DP are the same.

Also these results confirm the selection of the Thevenin EC model for its trade-off between complexity, observed performance, ease and quality of the actual final estimation provided by the heuristic algorithm. This choice is inline with what observed by [21] and [22] in [21,22] with a similar estimation procedure also based on a meta-heuristics technique. This suggestion is further strengthened in the following section related to the validation of the model.

Fig. 10 provides an additional hint for a final observation on the performance of the cost function with a focus on the Thevenin model. Fig. 10 actually proposes the trend of the NRMSE along the SoC domain and, therefore, the NRMSE of the model estimated in the single SoC range. A preliminary evaluation confirms the worse behaviour already highlighted in the upper and lower regions of the SoC domain. To mitigate this issue, the measurements are performed with a non-equal width of the SoC ranges in the extreme regions of the SoC domain as described in Section 3.3. A piece-wise model allows indeed to force a higher modelling density in more critical regions. By doing so, more models (even if characterised by a lower estimation accuracy) represent those critical regions of the battery characteristics. Those regions still present lower model performances with additional outliers suggested from the peaks in the NRMSE values (Fig. 10) and jumps in the values of parameters (Figs. 7 and 8). Nevertheless, we accept these performances as, from a practical point of view, any battery should not operate in deep discharge conditions [26]. The ordinary operation of the device is typically suggested by the manufacturer’s prescription of the depth discharge DoD and normal operation ranges (SoC and other electrical parameters). Such values ensure the operation within safety conditions and improve the life expectancy of the device.

A final discussion follows regarding the time required for the estimation calculations. Table 6 reports the parameter estimation time for a single SoC range (that is a single ECM model) with the provided implementation of the proposed procedure. The estimation time is upper-bounded for the current work by the specified maximum generation number imposed as one of the two termination criteria for the genetic algorithm. Moreover, more complex mathematical models require a higher number of parameters to be estimated and therefore longer computational time.

Nonetheless, the experienced times are small for all combinations exposed in Table 6. This holds despite the fine sampling time (0.1s) used in the subset of data that enters the estimation procedure. The reported values are strictly connected to the provided implementation, case study, coding language, and hardware platform. 3
3 PC equipped with an AMD Ryzen™ 9 5900X 12C/24T microprocessor, 32 GB of RAM, Linux (Ubuntu 20.04) Finally, more complex models (Thevenin and DP) benefit particularly from the parallelisation of the computations implemented in the underlying code.

The resulting models are validated on all the datasets of Table 2. For validation purposes, the datasets are adopted entirely (not slicing to extract the pulses are applied) with a downsampling to 1s timestamp where required due to the original sampling rate. This time-step let still appreciate the battery characteristic for the pulses applied in the HPPC test, while has no substantial impact on the profile of the CHADIS test performed in Constant Current (CC) condition.

The Table 7 reports the NRMSE value computed on the entire profile of each dataset. Most of the points of the HPPC original datasets belong to the idle time, therefore the reported NRMSE is mostly connected to the idle time of the battery and the modelling of the
U
O C
characteristic. By considering the data from the whole HPPC test, most of the distance measured from the true value is related to an error in the rest battery state, that is an incorrect selection of the adopted SoC range value with a consequent offset in the
U
O C
value. According to that analysis, it is clear the expected role paid by a correct SoC estimation as the model parameters are piece-wise functions of the SoC value. The model parameters are extracted from the look-up table and used to run the ECM model according to SoC, therefore the SoC estimation issue remains critical.

A final remark highlights the change of the sub-model that it is possible to experience in the validation phase and in any general simulation. In that case, the continuity of the model is enforced by computing a proper initial condition for the model with the new parameters at the jump from one SoC range to the other. While for the first-order model (the Thevenin model) this operation is straightforward, for the double polarisation DP EC model the identification of the initial conditions is less trivial and instability or jumps may occur in the change of sub-model according also to the distance between the estimated parameters of the two sub-models. Also [22] in [22] report fluctuation and, consequently, significant errors associated with more complex models, which require extra care in being properly tuned to be sufficiently robust during model execution.

In the current work, the less improvements in the performance of the more complex model (DP) may both derive from the nature of the estimation algorithm, which requires a higher number of parameters to be estimated for a complex model, and from the provided price-wise mathematical implementation of the model. While this approach should remove the requirement of the separate modelling of the parameters as a function of SoC, it still remains a rough approximation of a continuous characteristic with a non-trivial management of the transition between each sub-model specifically tuned on one SoC range (this is mostly true for the Open Circuit Voltage). The “piece-wise” nature of the model allows a simplification and a reduction of the modelling complexity while providing sufficiently good results for the desired applications.

The CHADIS dataset is adopted in this work as an alternative validation reference for the estimated models. This is done thanks to the different profile that characterises this test w.r.t. the HPPC ones. The NRMSE has in general a higher value in the case validated on those tests. Nonetheless, the comparison among performance values should be restricted to the first column, therefore focusing only on the performance experienced with the validation done with the CHADIS dataset. Due to its different nature, the CHADIS dataset may represent a more realistic profile of the battery general operation. As highlighted above, the Thevenin model that was estimated on the id3 dataset has the best performance.

This work proposes a new procedure for the estimation of parameters of battery equivalent circuit models using a data-driven approach and heuristic optimisation techniques. The work exploits the availability of several sets of measurement data deriving from a proper test campaign that adopts the most common and significative characterisation tests, that are carried under different parameters (focusing on the variation of the C-rate). The data allows us to tune the procedure in all its steps from the raw large datasets to the final use of the estimated model. Moreover, the developed procedure lends itself to be easily automated (with a focus on the data preparation and actual parameter estimation) and its application extended to potential further technologies and measurement datasets. The explored technology (LTO) is known to operate at high currents (we will explore till 3C charge and discharge operations) and high depth of discharge (DoD). Test the methodology in such an extreme condition (not common to all LiB technologies) may validate the operation of the established EC-based modelling also in these extreme regions of operation. A good modelling capability may finally bring to the exploration of an extension of the operative DoD also for the other battery technologies. This work explores an high number of combinations of models and C-Rates, but aims at highlighting a single option to be potentially applied to other technology and materials. According to these outcomes, it should be possible to apply the methodology of the current model to newer devices and materials only considering a reduced set of EC models and with only one value of C-rates, the one that resulted as best performing in the validation discussed above.

The results suggest the final adoption indication of the Thevenin model given the reviewed performance within the framework of the proposed procedure and the actual case study. This choice is driven by its trade-off between complexity, observed performance, and ease and quality of the actual final estimation provided by the heuristic algorithm. This work highlights and explores both the strengths and limitations encountered in the application of the outlined procedure to the specific case study. The final product of the estimation is a look-up table containing model parameter values estimated over discrete ranges of SoCs. The dependence of the parameters from the SoC value is a peculiar characteristic of the model. This approach mirrors the observation of the actual battery behaviour for various SoC values but poses implementation challenges from the modelling perspective. Such challenges need further research effort in the statement and execution of the optimisation problem.

Enrico Dalla Maria: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Mattia Dallapiccola: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Validation, Writing – original draft. Davide Aloisio: Conceptualization, Validation, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Giovanni Brunaccini: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Francesco Sergi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. David Moser: Writing – review & editing. Grazia Barchi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This paper was inspired by fruitful discussions during the collaboration in the European Union’s Horizon 2020 research and innovation programme under grant agreement No 768824 (HYBUILD).
The authors thank the Department of Innovation, Research and University of the Autonomous Province of Bozen/Bolzano for covering the Open Access publication costs.
